summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the applicant sought that the respondent pay his costs – Where the respondent filed no submissions in relation to costs – Where the court is persuaded that the circumstances justify the making of an order that the respondent pay some of the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the property adjustment aspect of the proceedings – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought costs by way of contribution in respect of a single expert report – Where the court is persuaded such an order is just and appropriate.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HOGAN J:

On 1 November 2024, for the reasons delivered that day,[1] I made orders finalising the parenting and property proceedings commenced by the Initiating Application filed 22 April 2021 and amended on 6 June 2023. The orders also made provision for the parties to apply for an order for costs.
Both Mr Arata (the applicant) and the Independent Children's Lawyer sought orders that Ms Rex (the respondent) pay their respective costs. Whilst the respondent has not filed any submissions in relation to this issue, I have proceeded on the basis that she opposes the making of any order requiring her to pay any of the costs of either the applicant or the Independent Children's Lawyer.
The respondent filed a Notice of Appeal on 28 November 2024 against the final orders made on 1 November 2024. She then filed an Application in a Proceeding, on 5 December 2024, seeking an order staying the operation of orders 1-28 and 30-51 of the final property settlement and parenting orders made on 1 November 2024 pending the determination of the appeal; the application was listed for hearing on 18 December 2024.
On 13 December 2024, Aldridge J, exercising the appellate jurisdiction of this Court, struck out the respondent’s Notice of Appeal filed 28 November 2024, and granted her leave to file an Amended Notice of Appeal on or before 21 February 2025. As well as making other orders, His Honour listed the matter for further directions on 24 February 2025.
In the Response to Application in a Proceedings filed by the applicant on 17 December 2024, the applicant sought that the respondent’s application for a stay of the operation of the orders be dismissed; he also sought that she pay his costs of and incidental to the application.
On 19 December 2024, orders were made (as amended on 20 December 2024) dismissing the application for a stay of the operation of orders 1-28 and 30-51 made on 1 November 2024 pending the determination of the appeal. Order 2 of the Orders made on 19 December 2024 provided that, in the event that any party sought an order that another party pay the costs of and incidental to the application for stay, any such party file and serve any affidavit and submissions in support of the application for a costs order within 28 days of the finalisation of the appeal.
The respondent’s appeal in relation to the orders made on 1 November 2024 was dismissed by the Full Court on 16 July 2025. The Full Court also ordered that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs fixed in the amount of $36,040 within 28 days.
Whilst other applications have been filed by the respondent, none appear to relate to the applications made by the applicant and the Independent Children's Lawyer for orders that the respondent pay costs.

Relevant principles and the application of the same

The starting point in relation to costs under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) is that each party bears their own costs. However, if it is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the Court may make such order as it considers just.
In considering what order, if any, as to costs should be made, the Court must have regard to the relevant matters prescribed under the Act. The Court may give such weight as it considers appropriate to any relevant factor.[2] It is well settled that no single factor has priority, nor must more than one factor be satisfied; rather, any one factor may be sufficient for the Court to be satisfied that the circumstances justify the making of an order as to costs.[3]
Consequently, the first question to be determined is whether the circumstances here justify departing from the statutorily prescribed starting point of each party bearing their own costs. If the discretion is exercised in favour of such departure, then it is necessary to consider the form of the order that is just.

The Applicant’s application for costs

By submissions filed on 28 November 2024, the applicant sought an order that the respondent pay his costs as follows:
(a) on an indemnity basis:
(i) of and incidental to the proceedings for interim orders sought in the Amended Application for Final Orders filed 6 June 2023; and

(ii) of and incidental to the adjournment of the first day of the final hearing on 8 April 2024; and

(iii) of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed 26 August 2024 and the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 13 September 2024; and

(b) on the party/party basis for the period 22 April 2021 to 27 September 2024 (excluding the matters set out above):

(i) in an amount agreed between the parties within 14 days or, failing agreement, as assessed; or alternatively,

(ii) fixed in the amount of $275,000; or

(c) alternatively to the assessment of costs described above: costs fixed in the amount of $300,000.

As noted earlier, no submissions about costs have been filed by or on behalf of the respondent.

Legal aid funding

Neither the applicant nor the respondent was in receipt of Legal Aid. Both engaged privately funded legal representation.

The parties’ respective financial circumstances

The applicant remains self-employed within his software company, Z Inc. He described his income as comfortable, albeit unpredictable. On the evidence he relied upon, his solicitors have rendered invoices, as at 27 September 2024, in the total amount of $609,244.[4]
The respondent is currently unemployed. It was submitted on the applicant’s behalf that, while she has the capacity for employment, she has not chosen to seek employment. By reference to the Costs Notice filed by the respondent’s former solicitors,[5] invoices had been rendered to her, as at 15 May 2024, in the amount of $482,201.47 including GST.[6]
I accept that both parties have private litigation funding loans which have been secured over real property which is likely to have to be sold. I accept that, alongside his litigation funding loan from PR Financial Services in the amount of approximately $186,000 (being principal borrowings of $150,000), the applicant has unpaid invoices in the amount of $401,707 (exclusive of interest) owing to his solicitors. Although there is no evidence before me about the current liability, I accept that, as at the time of the trial, the respondent had a litigation funding loan from R Pty Ltd in the amount of $208,000.[7] I note that, in her submissions on his behalf, the applicant’s solicitor contended that the liability pursuant to that loan was more than $400,000.
I accept the submission that the applicant spends about $62,000 of his income on rent per annum, whilst the respondent has continued – at least in the period from when the applicant moved off the property – to live on the Town E property, unencumbered by a mortgage. I also accept that it is much more likely than not that the applicant will continue to be entirely responsible for the financial costs associated with caring for the parties’ youngest child who lives with him – such conclusion formed part of the basis for the five per cent adjustment made in his favour in the disposition of the property settlement aspect of the proceedings.
I consider that the applicant’s income earning capacity exceeds that of the respondent; given this, his prospects of recovering financially – at least somewhat – from the enormous financial impost these proceeding have had on him are likely to be significantly better than the respondent’s prospects of recovering from the financial impost on her of the same.
However, as authority makes clear, impecuniosity (or even relative impecuniosity) is not, of itself, a bar to an order for costs being made where it is otherwise warranted.[8]

Whether any party has been wholly unsuccessful

The applicant submitted that the respondent had been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings and had also been wholly unsuccessful in relation to each application for interim orders that she made prior to and following the final hearing.
It was submitted on behalf of the applicant that the interim application he filed which came before Senior Judicial Registrar Thiele on 6 October 2023 was the result of the respondent’s failure to comply with earlier interim parenting orders made on 9 June 2022 and 21 March 2023. It was also submitted that the order which was ultimately made on 12 October 2023 had the effect of requiring the respondent to comply with those previous parenting orders – she was wholly unsuccessful in her attempt to obtain different interim parenting orders and, it was submitted, such failure should persuade the court of the existence of the exceptional circumstances required for the making of an order for costs on the indemnity basis.
The applicant also submitted that the court would be persuaded that the following matters constitute the exceptional circumstances required to be established before an order for costs is made on the indemnity basis:
(a) the respondent sought, on the first day of the trial, leave to read and rely upon a substantial affidavit (which comprised 205 pages and 215 pages of annexures) which was said to consolidate her previous trial material with matters which had occurred after that document had been sworn – such application saw the trial stood over until the following day; and

(b) on the final day of the four-day trial, the respondent’s solicitors unsuccessfully made repeated oral applications for the trial to be adjourned to enable the respondent to commission a forensic assessment of the corporate entities owned by the applicant; and

(c) after Judgment in the matter was reserved, the respondent made a number of applications to the Court:

(i) on 6 August 2024, she filed an Application in a Proceeding by which she sought that a Recovery Order issue for the child to return to her care – this application was dismissed; and

(ii) on 26 August 2024, she filed an Application in a Proceeding by which she unsuccessfully sought that:

(A) a recovery order issue in respect of the child; and
(B) a protection order be issued to safeguard the children; and
(C) the proceedings “be adjourned until all relevant financial details and matters related to the father’s legal disputes are fully disclosed and resolved”; and
(D) the finalisation of the property aspect of the proceedings be stayed; and
(E) the Court take into account the status of any ongoing investigation or legal matters involving the applicant; and

(iii) on 13 September 2024, she filed an Amended Application in a Proceeding by which she unsuccessfully sought:

(A) leave to reopen the proceedings to enable her to adduce further evidence pending the outcome of an ongoing police investigation into the applicant’s alleged fraudulent activities; and
(B) to be able to use documents from these proceedings in anticipated proceedings; and
(C) that the applicant be ordered to provide her with access to the books and records of all business entities in which she held an interest; and
(D) that the child be returned to her care.
In broad summary, the applicant submitted that the applications brought by the respondent had little to no prospects of succeeding. It was also submitted that the respondent had been wholly unsuccessful in her prosecution of the same and that they had included allegations of fraud on the applicant’s part which had never been made out by the respondent and which had not been put to the applicant when he was cross-examined at the trial; it was submitted that, given this, the court should consider such allegations to be vexatious.
Consequently, it was submitted on behalf of the applicant that the court would be persuaded that it was appropriate for the respondent to be ordered to pay the costs of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed on 26 August 2024 and the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 13 September 2024 on an indemnity basis.
Insofar as the application for costs related to the lengthy trial, it was submitted on behalf of the applicant, in essence, that the property orders which were made were at least equal to (if not more favourable to him) the property orders he had sought in the Initiating Application filed on 22 April 2021, and were broadly equivalent to those orders which he had sought in the Amended Initiating Application filed on 6 June 2023.

The conduct of the parties in relation to the proceedings and other relevant matters[9]

It was submitted on behalf of the applicant that the respondent’s conduct had unnecessarily increased his costs – it was submitted that such conduct involved engaging in prolific letter writing and the creation of affidavits of extraordinary length, as well as bringing numerous unmeritorious and ultimately unsuccessful interlocutory applications during the time the decision in the proceedings was reserved.
It was further submitted that the respondent had engaged in the following course of conduct throughout the proceedings, which was submitted to be particularly relevant to the determination of whether the circumstances justify the making of an order for costs against her:
(a) she had intended to, and did, prevent the child from spending time with the applicant; and

(b) she had unrelentingly asserted that the applicant was a violent person who had traumatised her and the children – a finding which was not made; and

(c) she had provided inconsistent evidence – for example, she had previously said she was content for the children to spend overnight time with the applicant if they lived in Country DD, then shortly thereafter had sought that the children spend only daytime time with him; on another occasion, after the applicant sought that the child spend time with him during school holidays (in the context of the child already spending four nights a fortnight in his care), she sought that the applicant’s time with the child be limited to supervised time only; and

(d) she had been dishonest in relation to her evidence that the maternal grandmother had provided more than $1 million in gifts during the course of the parties’ relationship – an assertion which was abandoned by the time her Case Outline was filed; and

(e) whilst she had made allegations against the applicant which were criminal in nature (namely, fraud), the applicant had not been cross-examined about these at the trial and she had not reported them to police until April 2024, following the completion of the hearing; and

(f) she made assertions about the value of the various companies but had provided no cogent evidence to support the same; and

(g) despite being on notice that there was independent evidence that did not support the parenting orders which she had proposed after the release of the first Family Report, she remained fixed in her position about the same; and

(h) just prior to the commencement of the trial, she had, without notice, taken control of a specific online account and listed various domain names for sale, which necessitated the applicant filing an Application in a Proceeding on 9 May 2024.

In essence, the applicant submitted that:
(a) the respondent’s conduct could be regarded as being, at best, a disingenuous and consistent course of conduct intended to frustrate and delay the proceedings and increase the costs of the same or, at worst, a deceitful, malicious and consistent course of conduct intended to frustrate and delay the proceedings and increase the costs of the same; and

(b) the consequence of the respondent’s conduct was that the proceedings, which were relatively straight forward, were made very difficult, arduous and extremely costly.

Whether an offer to settle the proceedings was made in writing and the terms of the same

I accept that, on 3 March 2023, the applicant made an offer to settle the proceedings on the basis that:[10]
(a) the respondent receive specified companies and agreement be reached that those companies have nil value; and

(b) another specified company be valued by a single expert, with the trial judge to determine how the costs of this were to be shared at the final hearing; and

(c) a joint letter of instruction to the proposed single expert agreed by 8 March 2023.

I accept that, on 7 February 2024, the applicant made an offer to settle the proceedings on the basis that:[11]
(a) the respondent pay him the sum of $800,000 within 42 days; and

(b) contemporaneously with the above transfer, he remove the caveat registered over the real property by his litigation lender and transfer his rights, title and interest in the real property to the respondent; and

(c) upon the transfer of the real property to her, the respondent indemnify the applicant in respect of all liabilities of and incidental to it, whether they already existed or arose in the future; and

(d) he transfer his shares and the whole of his right, title and interest in S Pty Ltd to the respondent, contemporaneously with the payment at (a) being made; and

(e) upon the transfer of S Pty Ltd, the respondent indemnify him in respect of all liabilities of or incidental to it, whether they already existed or arose in the future; and

(f) he transfer his right, title and interest to all and any of the domain names registered with a particular site contemporaneously with the payment at (a) being made; and

(g) subject to the preceding paragraphs, each party respectively be declared to have the sole right, title and interest to all property of whatever kind and nature, and all superannuation, in their sole name or sole respective possession.

I accept that, after the applicant made the offer on 7 February 2024, his solicitor sent correspondence to the respondent – such correspondence outlined the following to assist the respondent in her consideration of the settlement proposal:[12]
(a) there was clear evidence in the applicant’s affidavit that gifts which the respondent alleged her mother had made to the parties (exceeding $1 million) were not gifts but, save in relation to some small loans which the parties had repaid, were the parties’ own funds – and that there may be significant cost consequences for her if his evidence in this respect was accepted; and

(b) the applicant conceded that she (the respondent) had contributed $500,000 from her inheritance (received some time after the parties’ divorce) for the parties’ joint benefit and that she had also met personal expenses using the inherited funds; and

(c) the applicant had made a number of post-separation contributions which needed to be considered, such as:

(i) he had worked in a specified company and had generated income which was applied to the construction of a specified building and to paying related real property costs in the amount of approximately $125,544; and

(ii) he had contributed funds of at least $42,760 to the parties’ joint account; and

(iii) he had spent 12 months working on the real property in tasks associated with the construction of a specified building on the real property; and

(iv) he had solely cared for the maternal grandmother during her terminal illness; and

(v) he had spent $38,000 on improvements to a cabin located the real property; and

(vi) he had paid rent since April 2021 whilst the respondent had continued to live rent-free on the real property.

(d) the respondent had had the benefit of earning an income from the real property after separation and it had housed her and her husband and their child; and

(e) he had been the primary contributor to a specified company and to the companies which he had incorporated after separation such that he did not anticipate that the court would accept that she (the respondent) had made anything more than a modest direct contribution to it and that such contribution appeared to have ceased in 2009.

I accept that, on 8 February 2024, the applicant made an offer to settle the proceedings on the basis that:[13]
(a) within 42 days of the orders, the respondent vacate (and cause all other occupants to vacate) the real property; and

(b) within 42 days of the orders, he pay $150,000 to the respondent’s R Pty Ltd loan (on the basis that was less than what was required to discharge the loan) and, contemporaneous with the payment being made, the respondent do all things necessary to cause R Pty Ltd to withdraw the caveat it had lodged over the title to the real property; and

(c) within 42 days of the orders, and provided that he had made the payment in accordance with (b), the respondent do all acts necessary to transfer her rights, title and interest in the real property to him, free from all registered encumbrances; and

(d) contemporaneous with the transfer of the real property to him, he would do all acts necessary to facilitate and achieve the withdrawal of the caveat registered over the property by his litigation lender, RR Financial Services; and

(e) contemporaneous with the respondent’s compliance with (a)-(c), he would transfer all his rights, title and interest (including his shares) in specified companies to the respondent; and

(f) during the period until transfer of the companies, he would continue to work for them and operate them diligently; and

(g) contemporaneous with the respondent’s compliance with (a)-(c), he would transfer all rights, title and interest to all of the domain names registered with a specified site; and

(h) from the date of transfer by him to the respondent in accordance with (e)-(g), the respondent would indemnify him in respect of all liabilities of the companies and the nominated domain names, whether such liabilities existed now or arose in the future; and

(i) from the date of the respondent’s transfer of the real property to him in accordance with (c) and subject to her compliance with (b), he would indemnify her in relation to all liabilities of or incidental to the real property, whether such liabilities existed then or arose in the future; and

(j) he would execute a Deed of Restraint which would prohibit him from acting in competition with any of all of the specified companies for a period of two years from sale or transfer.

Clearly, none of these offers were accepted.
Given that the terms of the final order required the respondent to pay $1,043,248 to the applicant (and discharge all outstanding Council arrears) in exchange for his transfer to her of all his rights in the real property, it is clear that the respondent would have been better placed than she now is if she had accepted the 7 February 2024 offer.
It was submitted on behalf of the applicant that the February 2024 offer was so favourable to the respondent that her nonacceptance of it might be viewed as impudent.

Further discussion and conclusions

As was quite properly recognised in the submissions prepared on behalf of the applicant, the final orders provided for an equal division of the property of the parties. Whilst this outcome was that which the applicant had proposed when the proceedings commenced in April 2021, it was not the position that he took at trial: when his Case Outline document was filed on 2 April 2024, he contended that the court would be persuaded that it was just and equitable for him to receive property valued at 55 per cent of the net value of the property of the parties.
Consequently, the respondent did better at trial in relation to the property aspect of the proceedings than she would have done if she had determined to resolve that aspect of the proceedings on the basis of an acceptance of the position advice by the applicant at trial. However, she did worse at trial than she would have done if she had accepted the offer made by the applicant in early February 2024 and, measured in percentage terms, she achieved what the applicant had first proposed when the proceedings commenced – namely, an equal division of the property of the parties.
However, these parents were also engaged in a parenting dispute which ultimately saw a change made, on a final basis, to their youngest child’s parenting regime in favour of the applicant.
By virtue of the combination of:
(a) the respondent’s complete lack of success in the proceedings; and

(b) the manner in which the respondent chose to conduct the proceedings, including by making the applications that she did during the time that the decision was reserved and by advancing the contentions of alleged fraud by the applicant absent any proof of the same; and

(c) the respondent’s failure to accept the applicant’s initial suggestion of an equal property division and his subsequent attempts to formulate the same in a number of different ways (which I consider provided the respondent with various options about the manner in which the property of the parties would be divided between them) and her subsequent failure to accept the applicant’s 7 February 2024 offer of settlement (which provided that, upon making a payment to him of $800,000, she would receive his interest in the real property, his shares in a nominated company and a transfer of all domain names associated with a nominated site),

I consider that the circumstances here justify the making of an order that the respondent pay some of the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the property adjustment aspect of the proceedings and that it is just that she do so.

I am not, though, persuaded that the circumstances justify the making of an order that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the parenting aspects of the proceedings: both parents clearly love their child; both genuinely advanced their competing proposals; that the respondent failed in her attempts to persuade that the parenting orders she sought were in the child’s best interests and failed to persuade that the parenting orders sought by the applicant were not in the child’s best interests, does not of itself persuade me that the circumstances justify departing from the legislative starting point, a starting point which no doubt reflects the legislature’s view that parents should not be prevented, by fear of adverse costs orders, from advancing their competing positions that the orders they propose for their child’s future care are the orders which are in that child’s best interests.
The conclusion that the circumstances justify the respondent paying some of the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the property aspect of the proceedings does not, though, determine the basis on which such costs should be calculated.
Well-known authority makes it clear that, unless there are exceptional circumstances, an order for costs should be made on the party and party basis; I accept that to order costs to be paid on an indemnity basis is something which is a “very great departure” from the “normal standard” in this and other jurisdictions.[14] I also accept that the categories of circumstances which enliven the discretion to award indemnity costs are not closed.[15]
However, in Colgate-Palmolive Company v Cussons Pty Limited,[16] Shepherd J provided some examples of circumstances that might justify the awarding of indemnity costs – usefully, Holden CJ in Munday v Bowman,[17] drew from his Honour’s decision the following:
(a) where it appears that an action has been commenced or continued in circumstances where a party properly advised should have known that he had no chance of success. In such cases the action must be presumed to have been commenced or continued for some ulterior motive or because of some wilful disregard of the known facts; and

(b) making allegations of fraud, knowing them to be false, and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud; and

(c) evidence of particular misconduct causing loss of time to the court and to other parties; and

(d) the making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions; and

(e) an imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise.

It is clear that a number of these circumstances exist in this case.
After much reflection, I consider that it is just that the respondent pay the applicant’s costs of the property aspect of the proceedings on an indemnity basis for the period from 14 February 2024 (being a week after the 7 February 2024 offer was made to her) and 2 April 2024 (when the applicant filed a Case Outline by which he proposed that the just and equitable orders in the circumstances of the case were orders which would see him receive property valued at 55 per cent of the total net value of the property of the parties).
Given that I completely and unreservedly accept that any order for costs which would require these parties to reach agreement about the quantum of the same would be futile and that the inevitable consequence would be that they would be required to undertake the assessment process provided by the relevant Rules of the court, I am easily persuaded that the most appropriate course is that the amount of costs to be paid by the respondent to the applicant for the above should be fixed.
Given that the evidence suggests that the applicant’s litigation funder paid invoices totalling $28,632.72 in February and April 2024, I consider that such amount is appropriate to reflect the likely costs incurred by the applicant during this period of time.
I am also persuaded that it is just in the circumstances of this case that the respondent pay, on an indemnity basis, the applicant’s costs:
(a) thrown away as a consequence of her ultimately successful application on the first day of the trial to rely on a large affidavit only recently provided to the applicant’s lawyers; and

(b) of and incidental to the Application in a Proceeding filed 26 August 2024 insofar as the relief there sought related to the property adjustment aspect of the proceedings; and

(c) of and incidental to the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 13 September 2024 insofar as the relief there sought related to the property adjustment aspect of the proceedings, including the application for leave to reopen the proceedings to adduce evidence, the application for an order that the father provide her with access to specified financial accounts and that she have leave to use documents obtained in the course of these proceedings in furtherance of her attempts to have the father criminally prosecuted.

Given my conclusion about the appropriateness of fixing an amount of costs in this matter and my interpretation of the applicant’s alternative proposal about the quantification of costs, it seems to me that the costs sought on a fixed basis (calculated on the indemnity basis) in relation to the matters discussed in paragraph 49 is $25,000. Even if I am wrong in my appreciation of the applicant’s proposal, I consider that it is appropriate and just that the respondent be required to pay this amount to the applicant in relation to the costs of these matters.
Whilst others may disagree, I am not persuaded that it is just to simply order that, in addition to these costs, the respondent pay all of the applicant’s costs of and incidental to the property aspects of the proceedings on a party and party basis for the period from 22 April 2021 until 27 September 2024. I arrive at this conclusion because, whilst the applicant originally advanced that the just and equitable resolution of the property aspect of the proceedings would see an equal division of the property between the parties – and this was the final determination – his position at trial was to seek property valued at 55 per cent of the total property. It is, though, just in all the circumstances of this case, that the respondent be required to pay some of the applicant’s costs of the property aspect of the proceedings on the party and party basis because I am persuaded that her conduct during the same (including having counsel cross-examine the single expert witness about the value of the companies in circumstances where such cross-examination failed to change the expert opinion in any way and maintaining assertions about the value of the companies in the absence of any evidence to support such assertions) contributed to the significant costs incurred by both of these parties.
As noted above, the applicant’s application included that, if the court was not persuaded to order that the parties undertake the assessment process provided by the applicable Rules of court, it should simply fix the amount to be paid by the respondent in the sum of $275,000. This was said to be the amount referable to the applicant’s costs, on a party and party basis, for the proceedings from 22 April 2021 until 27 September 2024, exclusive of the costs associated with the applications referred to in paragraph 49 and an additional interim proceeding based on the interim orders sought by the applicant in an application filed 6 June 2023. I am not persuaded, having regard to the likely extent of the respondent’s indebtedness to her litigation funder, the consequent impost of this indebtedness on the amount she may receive from a sale of the real property and her obligation to the financial support of another child (being a matter relevant to the consideration of her financial circumstances), that it is just to order that the respondent pay costs in such an amount.
I accept that there is significant imprecision in the course I have decided to adopt; however, such imprecision is, in my view, preferable to requiring these parties to incur the costs associated with engaging in the costs assessment process. I apportion half of the $275,000 advanced by the applicant as representing his costs of and incidental to the entire proceedings (calculated on the party and party basis) in the period from 22 April 2021 until 27 September 2024 to the property aspect of the proceedings to arrive at the amount of $137,500. To reflect the conclusions expressed at paragraph 51 and to recognise the prospect of the disallowance, on an assessment, of some costs, I deduct the amount of $37,500 to arrive at the amount of $100,000.
In order to avoid the possibility of any double-counting, I deduct the amounts calculated on the indemnity basis to arrive at the sum of $46,367.28.
The consequence of the deliberations set out above is that, in the circumstances of this case and for the reasons expressed, I consider that the order as to costs which is just is an order that the respondent pay $100,000 to the applicant by way of costs, with such payment to be made within 30 days of the making of the order.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer’s application for a costs order

The Independent Children's Lawyer sought an order that the respondent pay $4,510.00 by way of contribution to the costs incurred in the preparation of the single expert report prepared by Dr P.
The matters relevant to the consideration of this application may be summarised to be as follows:
(a) on 12 October 2023, Judicial Registrar Thiele made orders that the parties obtain a single expert family report and that the parties each pay one-half of the single expert’s fees; and

(b) on 16 February 2024, Dr P prepared the family report; and

(c) shortly thereafter, the applicant paid his one-half share of the costs of obtaining the family report; and

(d) the respondent did not pay her one-half share of the costs of obtaining the family report because of the depletion of the funds provided by her litigation funder; and

(e) Dr P would not release the family report until her fees were paid in full; and

(f) given the above, Legal Aid New South Wales provided the funds needed to pay the respondent’s half-share of the costs of the family report on the proviso that the Independent Children’s Lawyer would seek an order to recover these funds.

In the circumstances set out, I consider it just and appropriate – either by way of order as to costs or in enforcement of the order made on 12 October 2023 – that the respondent pay the sum of $4,510 to Legal Aid New South Wales within 30 days of the making of the order.
